title: Classic pound cake
subtitle: One pound each of butter, sugar, flour, and eggs
excerpt: The original pound cake formula is a one-to-one ratio: equal weights of butter, sugar, flour, and eggs, with no other leavening. A small addition of baking powder and vanilla brings it in line with modern expectations without breaking the fundamental simplicity.
sourceNotes: The pound cake formula is documented in British and American cookbooks from the eighteenth century, including Hannah Glasse's The Art of Cookery (1747) and Eliza Leslie's Seventy-Five Receipts (1828), both now in the public domain. The formula name refers to one pound each of the four main ingredients.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze once fully cooled. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps at room temperature for 4 to 5 days, wrapped. The crumb tightens and improves after a day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 155 degrees C; the crack along the top is structural, not a fault

glossaryTerms.definition: The creaming method beats softened butter with sugar until the mixture becomes pale and noticeably increased in volume, trapping air bubbles that expand during baking and lift the cake. In a true pound cake this air is the only leavening; the mixture must be beaten for at least 5 minutes with an electric mixer before the eggs go in. Under-creaming is the most common reason a pound cake comes out dense.

content.text: The pound cake is one of the oldest cake formulas, and the name says it all: one pound each of butter, sugar, flour, and eggs. Modern versions add a little baking powder and vanilla, but the character comes from the
: butter and sugar beaten until pale, eggs added one at a time, flour folded in. The result is a dense, golden loaf with a crack down the top.

Preheat the oven to 175 degrees C (conventional). Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin. Beat the butter and sugar together with an electric mixer for at least 5 minutes, until the mixture is pale and has increased noticeably in volume. Add the eggs one at a time, beating for 30 seconds after each. Add the vanilla. If the mixture begins to look curdled, add a tablespoon of the measured flour to bring it back. Sift together the flour, baking powder, and salt, then fold into the batter in two additions, mixing only until the flour has just disappeared.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Bake for 55 to 65 minutes, until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the top is deep golden with a pronounced crack down the middle. The crack is normal and desirable. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Leave for at least 20 minutes before slicing; it cuts much more cleanly once cool.
